confidence.disclosure: Khwaja Shams al-Din Muhammad Hafez of Shiraz is the most beloved poet in the Persian language, but the historical record about his personal life is very thin. Almost nothing is recorded by his contemporaries, and his biography has to be reconstructed from internal references in his ghazals (which are notoriously difficult to date) and from much later, often legendary, tadhkira sources. The basic outline is clear: he spent essentially his entire life in Shiraz, lived through the volatile politics of the post-Mongol Mongol Injuids, the Muzaffarids, and the Timurid invasions, and his birth and death dates are reasonably firm. Specific events of his life are very loose.

sources: Hafez, Divan (the great body of his ghazals)
Dick Davis, Faces of Love (translations and introduction, 2012)

journey.event: Born around 1315 in Shiraz in the province of Fars in southern Iran, the same city that produced Saadi a century earlier. His family is of humble origins; his father is said to have been a coal merchant who died while Hafez was a child.

Loses his father in childhood. The young Hafez has to work in a bakery and other modest jobs to support his mother and brothers. Despite the poverty, he memorises the Quran completely - it is from this that he takes his pen name Hafiz, meaning 'one who has memorised the Quran'.

Receives a thorough classical education in Shiraz, mastering Arabic and Persian grammar, prosody, the religious sciences, and the Persian poetic tradition that goes back through Saadi to Sanai and Attar. Begins composing his own ghazals.

Lives through the chaotic politics of post-Mongol southern Iran. Shiraz is fought over by the Injuids, the Muzaffarids, and various local lords. Hafez attaches himself to a series of patrons - including Abu Ishaq Inju, who governs Shiraz from 1343 to 1353 and presides over a brilliant court culture - and writes panegyrics to them along with his ghazals.

In 1353 the Muzaffarid prince Mubariz al-Din Muhammad takes Shiraz from the Injuids and executes Abu Ishaq. Mubariz al-Din is a strict religious puritan who closes the wine houses and bans the open singing of ghazals. Hafez's poetry from this period is full of bitter complaints against the hypocrisy of the muhtasib (the religious censor).

In 1358 Mubariz al-Din is overthrown and blinded by his own son Shah Shuja, who becomes the new Muzaffarid ruler. Shah Shuja is an educated man who himself composes poetry and patronises Hafez generously. Hafez writes some of his most famous ghazals during the years of Shah Shuja's reign.

Lives through more political upheaval as Shah Shuja loses Shiraz to his brother Shah Mahmud (1364) and then regains it (1366), and as further internal struggles continue. Hafez navigates these changes with great skill, surviving and continuing to compose. He is sometimes in favour, sometimes out, but never moves from Shiraz.

Around 1376 makes a brief and unsuccessful trip to Yazd at the invitation of a patron there. Hates being away from Shiraz. According to one famous ghazal, even an invitation from the sultan of Bengal (an actual historical event - the Bengali sultan invited him east) cannot induce him to leave: 'All the parrots of India will become sugar-eating from this Persian sweet that travels to Bengal.'

In late 1387 Timur (Tamerlane) takes Shiraz for the first time. According to a famous (though probably apocryphal) anecdote, Timur summons Hafez and reproaches him for the line 'If that Turk of Shiraz would take my heart in her hand, for her Indian mole I would give Samarqand and Bukhara' - asking how a poet could give away the cities Timur had conquered with the sword. Hafez supposedly replies: 'Yes, sire, and it is from such extravagance that I am poor.'

Dies in his beloved Shiraz around 1390 (792 AH), aged about 75. He is buried in the northern part of the city in a garden he had loved during his life, the Musalla, on the bank of the Ruknabad stream that he so often celebrated in his verses. The present mausoleum, the Hafezieh, is the most-visited monument in Shiraz; many Iranians come daily to recite his poems at his grave and use his Divan as a book of divination (fal-e Hafez).
